data.title: SA analyst upgrades/downgrades: MU, ORCL, SMCI, LTM
data.description:
Oracle (ORCL) and Micron (MU) have earned upgrades as analysts pivot bullish on the undeniable momentum of AI infrastructure and cloud growth. On the downside, LATAM Airlines (LTM) and Super Micro Computer (SMCI) face significant headwinds. Ricardo Fernandez downgraded LATAM from Buy to Sell, citing a potential 80% spike in jet fuel costs stemming from geopolitical conflict. Meanwhile, Envision Research downgraded Super Micro as intensifying legal and regulatory risks, including employee indictments and shareholder lawsuits, create systemic concerns that outweigh recent valuation contraction.

Upgrades

  • Oracle (ORCL): Upgrade Sell to Buy by Analysis Fundamental. The analyst points to a 24% stock decline as an improved entry point while highlighting massive growth in cloud and AI infrastructure segments, including multicloud database revenue surging 531% YoY.

“ORCL remains with negative FCF, and its backlog depends on OpenAI; however, Q3’26 results showed that the company, on the one hand, reduced OpenAI’s dependency and, on the other hand, proved that the OCI business accelerates the overall growth trajectory, as well as being a key component for cross-selling opportunities leading to customer stickiness.”

  • Micron Technology (MU): Upgrade Sell to Neutral by Julian Lin. Unprecedented revenue expansion driven by AI-related demand has forced a withdrawal of previous bearishness, though the analyst remains cautious about long-term cyclicality.

“It is becoming increasingly clear that the AI story is far from being played out, even if the AI companies have experienced historic valuation resets. … MU’s historical cyclicality tempers bullishness, capping potential upside, despite trading at under 4x FY27e earnings.”

Downgrades

  • LATAM Airlines Group (LTM): Downgrade Buy to Sell by Ricardo Fernandez. Rising jet fuel costs stemming from geopolitical conflict and potential currency risks are expected to severely impact the company’s aggressive growth plans and 2026 margins.

“The consensus forecasts revenue growth of 11% and an EBITDA margin of 27% for 2026, valuing the shares at $70 with an EV/EBITDA price target of 6x. Very much in line with guidance and global peers. The market has not taken into account the potential impact of fuel costs on the business model. It's very likely that LTM revises this guidance in the 1Q26 results report, or even preempts it with an earnings warning.”

  • Super Micro Computer (SMCI): Downgrade Buy to Sell by Envision Research. Intensifying legal and regulatory risks, including employee indictments and shareholder class actions, have created systemic concerns that outweigh the recent valuation contraction.

“I anticipate SMCI to be surrounded by intensifying legal and regulatory pressures in the foreseeable future, which is a systemic risk that can threaten SMCI’s foundational operations. These risks are only partially compensated by the valuation contraction since, thus leading me to lower my rating to hold.”

data.title: Trump on Iran: ‘Entire country can be taken out in one night, tomorrow night’
data.description:
U.S. President Donald Trump issued a stark warning to Iran celebrating a successful rescue mission, declaring that “the entire country can be taken out in one night, and that night might be tomorrow night.”

The threat came as Trump described the downing of an American F-15 fighter jet deep inside Iranian territory during Operation Epic Fury.

Trump said he immediately ordered the rescue despite the significant risks, stating, “We could have ended up with 100 dead as opposed to one or two,” but emphasized that “in the United States military, we leave no American behind.”

The rescue operation deployed 21 military aircraft into hostile Iranian airspace for seven hours, flying at low altitudes under heavy enemy fire.

“Nobody has the equipment that we have, and nobody has the military that we have, not even close,” Trump said, speaking at the White House, calling it “the most powerful military anywhere in the world by far.”

The pilot was extracted by an HH-60 Jolly Green II helicopter while facing close-range gunfire, while a second crew member, an injured weapons system officer, evaded the Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps by scaling “cliff faces, bleeding rather profusely” through treacherous mountain terrain before signaling his location using a sophisticated beacon device.

Trump called the mission “very historic,” noting that such complex rescues are rarely attempted in combat zones and that despite the extreme danger, no American was injured.

“This is a rescue that’s very historic,” he said. “It’ll go down in the books.”

More on the U.S.-Iran war

  • U.S.-Iran headed towards path of escalation – Rapidan Energy’s Scott Modell
  • Prediction markets push back odds of Iran-Israel-U.S. conflict ending soon
  • China reserves offshore airspace for 40 days - report
  • U.S., Iran and regional mediators discuss 45-day ceasefire - report
  • Gold slides as Trump escalates Iran threats, inflation fears rise

data.title: Meta may open source versions of its upcoming AI models: report
data.description:
Meta Platforms (META) is preparing to release the first new AI models developed under Alexandr Wang and plans to eventually offer versions of these models through an open source license, Axios reported.

Before openly releasing versions of the new models, the U.S. tech giant intends to keep some pieces proprietary, and to ensure they do not add new levels of safety risk, the report added, citing sources.

Last year in June, Meta announced a $14.3B investment in AI startup Scale, acquiring a 49% stake. Through the deal, Wang joined Meta to head its “superintelligence” team focused on artificial general intelligence, or AGI.

Meta has said the first family of models is aimed at helping it catch up to rivals after its last Llama 4 family fell behind, with an aim that future models can lead the industry. AI giants OpenAI (OPENAI) and Anthropic (ANTHRO) have hinted that their next models are expected soon, the report noted.

Meta knows its new models may not be competitive across the board with the coming ones from those companies but believes it will have areas of strength that appeal to consumers, according to the report.

Wang has indicated that some of its largest new models will remain proprietary — a shift toward a more hybrid strategy, the report noted.

data.title: Deutsche Bank, RBC participate in financing for Eat Happy-Hana Group deal - report
data.description:
Wall Street banks are lending €750M ($867M) to finance the ~ €1.5B merger of Asian food company Eat Happy and the European division of sushi supplier Hana Group SAS, according to a media report on Monday.

The deal includes a  €650M term loan and a  €100M revolving credit facility from Deutsche Bank (DB), RBC Capital Markets (RY), and UniCredit (UNCFF)(UNCRY), Bloomberg reported, citing people familiar with the matter. The banks can either sell the debt to investors as leveraged loans or as high-yield bonds, depending on market conditions, the people said.

One Rock Capital Partners, a middle-market private equity firm based in New York, will make a strategic investment in the combination that will form a platform for fresh Asian convenience food in Europe, it said in a statement when the deal was announced on April 1.

The transaction follows other notable M&A-related financings, such as a $20B financing for the buyout of Electronic Arts (EA) and a $7.2B offering for bubble-wrap producer Sealed Air (SEE).

data.title: Playtika rallies on buyout buzz as turnaround traction likely to lure bargain hunters
data.description:
Playtika (PLTK) shares have been jolted out of a months-long sideways trade after the company  disclosed that a special committee has been formed to consider “strategic alternatives" across its portfolio.

The development was widely viewed as a search for a buyer, though there are no assurances of this outcome since it is the second time since 2022 that the company has embarked on a strategic review.

However, if the company is looking for a suitor, it shouldn’t have trouble finding a buyer given its strong finish to 2025. Playtika (PLTK) reported record free cash flow, a solid profit margin, record direct-to-consumer contribution (now contributing more than a third to its revenue), and new revenue-generating games.

Additionally, strong player retention and the growing value of its direct-to-consumer users should enhance its appeal to a potential buyer. While its slots portfolio is losing traction, it's more than offset by strength in SuperPlay, Bingo Blitz, and Disney Solitaire, which continue to support resilient cash flow and shareholder returns.

But with shares down more than 35% year-over-year, why is investor sentiment so negative? Seeking Alpha analysts argue that Playtika’s (PLTK) value understates its fundamentals…a good setup for a buyer.

“Valuation is way out of sync with what’s actually happening in the business. Using the consensus 2026 EPS of $0.57 and a pretty basic 10 to 12 times multiple, you get a fair value between $5.70 and $7.08,” says Seeking Alpha analyst Motti Sapir.  That’s about 108% to 158% higher than Friday’s closing price. “Wall Street’s missing how much cash these games keep making and how the new titles might really move the needle,” Sapir adds.

Agrees Seeking Alpha analyst Ricardo Weder. “Playtika is executing a successful pivot of legacy social casino games to a high-growth casual games platform, creating an undervalued opportunity.”

According to Weder, Playtika (PLTK) is making a massive pivot from their old “cash cow games to turbocharge a new fleet of high-growth games.”

“This isn’t just business as usual; it is a calculated and high-stakes game of its own, and the latest data suggests they might just be winning,” he adds.

So why is the company’s stock trading like a business in decline?

One reason is the 840 basis point margin compression between 2023 and Q2 2025, which Weder discounts as more of a sign of capital deployment than operational failure, as management sacrifices short-term profitability for investment in high-growth areas. 

There was also substantial debt at the end of 2024 of $2.4B, which also spooked investors. But thanks to consistent free cash flow, extending debt maturities, and “mitigating debt-related risks,” Playtika (PLTK) has been “actively managing its liabilities to support its long-term strategic goals,” says Weder.

Sapir argues that Playtika is attractive for investors willing to bet on a turnaround and wait for the market to recognize its underlying value—a view that could just as easily apply to a potential acquirer.

data.title: Embraer CFO steps down as CEO takes interim finance role
data.description:
Brazilian aircraft manufacturer Embraer (EMBJ) said Monday that Chief Financial Officer Antonio Carlos Garcia has stepped down from his role.

The company added that Chief Executive Officer Francisco Gomes Neto will temporarily take over the finance chief’s responsibilities while a successor is identified.

Shares of Embraer (EMBJ) were little changed Monday.

data.title: Intel in talks with Amazon, Google for packaging services: report
data.description:
Intel (INTC) has been in ongoing discussions with at least two large customers, including Amazon (AMZN) and Google (GOOG) (GOOGL), for its advanced packaging services, Wired reported, citing multiple sources.

Intel’s ambitions for its advanced packaging business depend largely on whether it secures outside customers, such as these tech giants, the report added.

Intel noted in an email to Seeking Alpha that it cannot comment on the rumors. Amazon and Google did not immediately respond to a request for comment from Seeking Alpha.

One former Intel employee with knowledge of the company’s packaging efforts told the news outlet that Intel’s EMIB and EMIB-T are designed to be a more “surgical” way of packaging chips than Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing's (TSM) approach, the report noted.

This method is supposed to be more power efficient, save space, and, ideally, save customers money in the long run. Intel said that EMIB-T will roll out in fabs this year, according to the report.

AI has been a key catalyst for these changes. "Because of AI, advanced packaging has really come to the forefront," said Naga Chandrasekaran, head of Intel’s Foundry. "Even more so than the silicon itself, chip packaging is going to transform how this AI revolution comes to fruition over the next decade."

Intel started preparing for mass production of EMIB-T in Rio Rancho, New Mexico. The Rio Rancho facility has about 2700 Intel employees, around 200 fewer than it had last year. Intel CEO Lip-Bu Tan had reduced the company's workforce after he took over as CEO, the report added.

Katie Prouty, the Rio Rancho site plant manager, noted that one of Intel’s selling points for advanced packaging is that customers can opt to use Intel for any part of the process, or, “enter and exit the highway” at any point. For example, a customer can buy wafers from one entity, then come to Intel’s fabs for the next step; or contract with an outsourced semiconductor assembly and test company for traditional chip packaging, then use Intel for advanced packaging, the report noted.

"That's not something Intel did before. We never took in other customers’ wafers," said Prouty. "That’s been a huge mindset shift."

One former Intel employee, speaking on background, said that Intel’s target packaging customers may be hesitant to announce collaborations with Intel for two reasons. They are either waiting to see if the company can deliver on its fab expansion promises, or they are concerned TSM could potentially allocate fewer wafers to customers once they say they are using Intel for packaging. It is not the tech itself they would be taking a risk on, the former staff noted, adding that it is the broader market dynamics.

However, Chandrasekaran is more circumspect. “I think we want to be very disciplined around the idea of: We don’t talk about our customers. Successful foundries don’t say, ‘We have signed up these customers.’ We want the customers to talk about our product," said  Chandrasekaran.

Chandrasekaran said that a big indicator that the customers have arrived will be a notable jump in Intel Foundry's spending. “As we sign up these customers, we'll have to increase our capital expenditures,” said Chandrasekaran, adding that “and then the street will see it.”

Update: Intel declined to comment.

data.title: U.S.-Iran headed towards path of escalation – Rapidan Energy’s Scott Modell
data.description:
Scott Modell, CEO of Rapidan Energy Group, warned that the U.S. and Iran are heading toward a “path of escalation” as President Donald Trump’s deadline for reopening the Strait of Hormuz approaches.

In an interview with CNBC, Modell assessed the odds of a successful 45-day ceasefire deal at just 10% to 15%, citing “absolutist demands on both sides” that have led to entrenched, unworkable positions.

“I think we are headed on the path of escalation at this point,” Modell said.

Current oil supply disruptions stand at approximately 9M barrels per day, though Modell credited “heroic efforts” by Saudi Arabia and the UAE for managing redirects through the Fujairah and Yanbu terminals.

These redirects account for roughly 5M barrels of offsets, but Modell cautioned that they highlight “real vulnerabilities” in the global supply chain that could prove catastrophic if no diplomatic off-ramp is found.

Despite the elevated geopolitical risks, oil prices (CL1:COM), (CO1:COM) have remained relatively stable—a response Modell attributed to market complacency.

He explained that traders assume President Trump is “desperate to find an off-ramp” to avoid prolonged conflict, a sentiment Modell believes is accurate but potentially misguided.

“We have to escalate to de-escalate,” he said, noting that none of the fundamental goals established at the beginning of the standoff have been achieved.

If no deal materializes by tomorrow’s deadline and the administration proceeds with strikes on Iranian infrastructure, Modell identified two specific tail risks that could send prices soaring. Iranian or Houthi forces could target the Yanbu terminal in retaliation, and Modell warned that significant damage isn’t required to spook markets.

“All you have to do is hit a couple of storage tanks, and I think the market will start to realize that one glorious redirect… is very vulnerable,” he explained.

The most catastrophic scenario, according to Modell, involves Abqaiq—a Saudi crude processing facility that handles 7M barrels per day and is considered the most important oil facility in the world.

Iranian forces have already tested regional defenses with strikes on the Shaybah oilfield and the East-West pipeline, and unlike the 2019 attack on Abqaiq that served as a “shot across the bow,” any future strike designed to inflict real damage would be difficult to repair.

Mediation efforts by Pakistan and other nations have so far failed to bridge the gap between the two sides, and Modell suggested the market is underestimating the administration’s history of following through on threats.

More on the U.S.-Iran war

  • Prediction markets push back odds of Iran-Israel-U.S. conflict ending soon
  • China reserves offshore airspace for 40 days - report
  • U.S., Iran and regional mediators discuss 45-day ceasefire - report
  • Gold slides as Trump escalates Iran threats, inflation fears rise
  • Prediction markets signal a prolonged Middle East conflict as oil risks mount

data.title: The stock market has trigged its first ‘proper buy signal’ since Liberation Day – HSBC’s Kettner
data.description:
Max Kettner, chief multi-asset strategist at HSBC, believes the stock market (SP500), (COMP:IND), (DJI) has triggered its first proper buy signal since the Liberation Day episode, and he expects Monday’s closing low of $63.43 to hold as the near-term bottom.

The strategist sees bullish conditions emerging across the broader risk asset spectrum, including equities and credit spreads, and echoed sentiments by Yardeni Research’s Ed Yardeni, who said Monday was the market’s bottom and maintained his year-end S&P 500 (SP500) target of 7,700 despite recent volatility.

Speaking in an interview with CNBC, Kettner explained that while high-frequency data on labor markets and consumer spending remained solid throughout March, he had been wary due to market positioning. That changed last week when both systematic and discretionary positioning shifted dramatically.

“As of last week, both systematic positioning, but particularly discretionary positioning—particularly around the hedging side of things—has now really triggered the first proper buyer signal for the broader risk asset spectrum really since the liberation day episode,” Kettner said.

The bullish outlook is supported by multiple technical indicators and sentiment surveys. Kettner pointed to momentum signals, put-call ratios, skews, and investor sentiment surveys including AAII and Investor Intelligence as confirming the shift. He emphasized that investors are now heavily over-hedged across the board, a condition that historically supports a market bottom.

Despite the optimism, Kettner warned that the upcoming core CPI reading remains a critical factor for market stability. He suggested that a high reading of 0.4% or 0.5% would be “not great news” for the recent momentum.

HSBC utilizes what Kettner calls a “danger zone” model, identifying the 4.5% level on the 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) as a threshold where risk assets begin to struggle.

The market is currently only about 15 basis points away from this level, leaving what Kettner described as an uncomfortable margin for safety.

If a hot inflation print pushes yields into that danger zone, Kettner warned that virtually nothing would offer protection.

“Nothing works, whether it’s EM, whether it’s credit, whether it’s rates, whether it’s equities,” he explained, adding that such a scenario “will spell trouble indeed really for the entire asset class spectrum except for the greenback.”

data.title: Dollar eases as geopolitical uncertainty keeps markets cautious: Currency Recap
data.description:
The U.S. dollar was down on Monday, as investors assessed shifting geopolitical developments and cautious risk sentiment tied to the ongoing U.S.-Iran conflict.

The dollar index (DXY), which measures the greenback against a basket of major currencies, was last down 0.07% to $99.96 but remains up about 1.72% for the year to date.

Dollar weekly moves and key drivers:

Over the past week, the index slipped 0.29%, with the U.S. dollar largely supported earlier in the period by safe-haven demand as tensions in the Middle East escalated.

 

Concerns over a prolonged conflict, including threats of intensified strikes and continued disruption to the Strait of Hormuz, drove oil prices sharply higher, raising inflation risks and reinforcing demand for the dollar as a safe-haven asset.

The greenback also drew support as rising energy prices  and geopolitical uncertainty led markets to reassess the global interest rate outlook, with expectations for policy easing pushed further out.

However, sentiment shifted intermittently during the week, with the dollar weakening at times on signs of potential de-escalation, including ceasefire discussions and mixed messaging from U.S. officials, which helped ease risk aversion and supported risk-sensitive assets.

The resulting back-and-forth in geopolitical headlines kept currency markets volatile, with traders reacting to each shift in the outlook for the conflict while remaining cautious amid uncertainty over its duration and economic impact.

During the period, global markets were influenced by developments in the U.S.-Iran conflict, sharp swings in oil prices, evolving inflation expectations, and broader shifts in risk sentiment.

U.S. Treasury yields were mixed overall, with the 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) holding steady at 4.33% over the week, while the 2-year yield (US2Y) rose 4 basis points to 3.86%.

Currency Highlights:

Major currency movements (March 30 to April 06)

 

Euro (EUR:USD)

+0.43%

Pound Sterling (GBP:USD)

+0.05%

Japanese Yen (JPY:USD)

-0.16%

Chinese Yuan (CNY:USD)

+0.36%

Swiss Franc (CHF:USD)

-0.28%

Australian Dollar (AUD:USD)

+0.79%

Canadian Dollar (CAD:USD)

-0.09%
 

The Japanese Yen (JPY:USD) was last down 0.09%, with the currency easing as risk sentiment showed signs of stabilization despite ongoing geopolitical tensions.

During the week, the yen saw choppy moves, strengthening on heightened U.S.-Iran conflict risks and safe-haven demand, while pulling back on intermittent optimism around potential de-escalation.

The Chinese Yuan (CNY:USD) was last up 0.14%, with the currency supported as regional sentiment steadied despite ongoing geopolitical uncertainty.

During the week, the yuan tracked mixed developments in Asian markets and economic data, including signs of moderating manufacturing momentum and steady services activity, while broader risk sentiment remained tied to U.S.-Iran conflict headlines.

The Euro (EUR:USD) was last up 0.24%, with the currency holding gains as regional sentiment remained mixed amid ongoing geopolitical uncertainty. 

Over the week, the euro tracked developments in European markets and economic data, including rising inflation, higher unemployment, and fluctuating risk sentiment tied to U.S.-Iran conflict headlines.
